summary: Joel Spolsky describes the final days before shipping CityDesk's beta, noting that the team is on schedule thanks to estimates made months earlier. He contrasts the calm, quiet reality of a well-managed ship cycle with Hollywood's frantic last-minute coding trope. The post emphasizes that good teams run out of work before the deadline rather than scrambling to finish. He also argues strongly for reaching zero known bugs before releasing a beta, explaining that known bugs waste beta testers' time and goodwill.
key_points: Well-estimated schedules lead to quiet, calm ship days rather than last-minute scrambles
Teams that write code until the last minute produce poor-quality software
Reaching zero known bugs before a beta release is essential to get useful feedback from testers
Known bugs in a beta waste tester goodwill and prevent them from finding unknown issues
Regular schedule check-ins with revised estimates and feature cuts keep projects on track
money_quotes: On good teams, the days before shipping just get quieter and quieter as programmers literally run out of things to do one at a time.
No code works the way you expected it to work the first time, and if you're writing code up until the last minute, it's going to suck.
It is crucial to get to zero known bugs before releasing a beta.
It is ok to have bugs in the beta, just not known bugs.
If you don't, you'll waste a lot of time during the beta reading 200 emails about a bug that you already knew about.

A common misconception, I assume popularized by Hollywood, is that as you get closer to shipping software, activity becomes frenetic as everybody scrambles to finish all the things that need to be done in time for the deadline.
key_insight: A well-run software project gets quieter as the ship date approaches, and releasing a beta with zero known bugs is essential to getting valuable feedback from testers.
